---
title: "Machine Learning Concepts"
author: "Nikhita Purohit"
date: "2026-03-29"
format: html
categories: [Concept Understanding]
editor: source
---

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![](images/clipboard-3240568454.png)The ML concepts that I understand and the ones that I don't fully understand

### Concepts I Understand

1.  Sampling data and adding a seed to make sure the sample doesn't change even after running the code again.

2.  Reading, cleaning, visualizing data

3.  Linear regression: y = mx + c, where y is the dependent variable, m is the slope, x is the independent variable, and c is the y intercept

4.  Least squares: technique used to find the best-fitting curve or line for a set of data points. They are squared in order to get positive values. The aim of least squares is to minimize the error. RSS- residual sum of squares RSS = e1\^2 + e2\^2 + e3\^2 + ......... en\^2 where e is the difference between the regression value and the actual value TSS- total sum of squares

5.  In the linear regression summary, a higher R\^2 value is a sign that the model is accurate and good- captures more variance. R\^2 = (TSS-RSS)/TSS

6.  Stars next to the values indicate significance.

7.  RMSE- root mean squared error - find the mean of the difference squared- and square root it The model with the least RMSE is the best model/ most accurate model

8.  Creating custom functions

9.  Logistic regression uses glm - generalised linear model - binomial. Linear regression uses lm - if you use glm - gaussian

10. If you have multiple logistic regression models, lowest AIC value - best / more accurate model

11. type = "response" gives the probability

12. Confusion matrix -\> actual vs predicted values

13. Clustering iter.max- keep repeating the process 15 times wss- within sum of squares tot.withinss- means total error map is a function from purr it takes the k values 1 to 15 and uses it in the function wss

14. (between_SS / total_SS) if it's usually 70% and above that means it's a good model and was able to capture a lot of variance

### Concepts I Don't Fully Understand

1.  apply and tapply

2.  I understand how to interpret the results after a kmeans test, but I don't fully understand what each line of code does.
